artifacts.content:
Hi Javi,

I’m still at my desk at 300 Richards (third floor is a ghost town after 6), trying to wrap my head around tomorrow’s Oak Park walk, and I just needed to get this out of my system.

I keep looping back to those nights in March 2022 when I was walking from the Planning Commission meetings after 8:30 p.m., cutting past 34th and Broadway. That one night when that guy followed me and wouldn’t stop with the comments until I ducked into the 35th Street substation to give a statement to the Sacramento PD officer there is still in my body. Every time I’m drafting language about “upgraded exterior lighting” along the 34th Street corridor, I’m picturing that stretch and thinking about Elena waiting at the Oak Park Community Center after her program. I never want her—or any teenage girl—to feel what I felt on that corner.

It’s the same reason I keep the project so tightly scoped: repainting façades, swapping out doors and windows in the existing openings, fixing sidewalks, adding wall-mounted and under-awning lighting, planter boxes, that sort of thing. No new buildings, no added parking lots, nothing that ramps up traffic. After Elena’s asthma episode walking home by the corridor on 3/18/23, and all the time she spends near McClatchy, I promised you I wouldn’t be the planner who greenlights something that makes the air worse there.

Anyway, I’ll probably be home closer to 7:30. Can you handle dinner and remind Mateo to stick his soccer stuff by the door for tomorrow? I already pulled chicken out to thaw.

Besos,
M

metadata.subject: Why I keep obsessing about 34th Street lighting

artifacts.content:
Hey Carlos,

I was thinking about you today walking past La Esperanza on 34th. I grabbed a concha and coffee with Luis Ramirez again, the panadería owner I told you about. Back on 4/5/23 he admitted he was three months behind on his $2,400/month commercial rent and terrified the landlord would use the run-down façade and faded sign as an excuse not to renew his lease. That conversation has been in my head every time I talk about “supporting long-standing tenants” on this corridor.

Honestly, it’s not just Luis. I keep flashing back to Mamá’s alterations shop on Stockton Boulevard. When she had to close in June 2016 because she couldn’t qualify for that $15,000 line of credit—thanks to the old medical debt from her 2012 knee replacement at UC Davis Medical Center—it broke her. Watching her pack up the sewing machines still makes me angry. I don’t want the women-owned salon on 34th to end up like that just because its façade is dated and the sign keeps flickering.

And then there’s what happened to you on Franklin. When the building owner redeveloped that auto-detailing site in late 2019 and your $19/hour job just evaporated after six years, no relocation, nothing, and you had to move into our place at 2714 22nd for eight months while you pieced together gig work—that’s the picture in my mind when I say I’m trying to stabilize existing businesses, not clear them out for something shinier.

So when you hear me talk about “minor exterior improvements” and “no changes in use,” it’s all of that. It’s you, Mamá, Luis, and a whole corridor of people trying to hang on.

Love,
Mari

metadata.subject: Why this 34th St work feels so personal

artifacts.content:
Work/Personal Reflection – Oak Park 34th St

I need to keep reminding myself why I drew such a hard line on scope for the Oak Park Small Business Façade Improvement Project – 34th Street Corridor (3401–3459 34th, between 5th and 8th). It’s not just about ticking CEQA boxes: Class 1 (Existing Facilities, §15301), Class 2 (§15302), Class 3 (§15303). It’s about staying sane and protecting the people I love.

1) Legal scar tissue: After sitting through the February 7, 2022 settlement conference at the Gordon D. Schaber Sacramento County Courthouse, listening to the City Attorney’s Office walk through how Mark Delaney got personally dragged into that old CEQA challenge, I promised myself: no more gray-area ground disturbance in Oak Park. Keep it strictly repair/maintenance—repainting façades, replacing doors/windows in existing openings, resurfacing sidewalks, upgraded exterior lighting, small planters in already disturbed areas. No grading beyond shallow trenching for conduit and irrigation.

2) Family bandwidth: Since Mamá’s minor stroke at Kaiser South Sacramento on 4/29/24, I’ve been doing twice-weekly evening caregiving runs to her Franklin Boulevard apartment on top of a 40–45 hour workweek. I cannot take on a full EIR circus—months of hearings, late-night staff reports—without something else breaking.

3) Health promises: After Elena’s asthma attack walking home along the 34th Street corridor on 3/18/23, I told Javi I’d keep any corridor work I touch focused on maintenance and cosmetic upgrades only—no new traffic-generating uses, no extended construction windows that could worsen localized air quality around McClatchy High.

Bottom line: modest, targeted, defensible. Help existing small, often minority- and women-owned businesses stay put, without putting my family or myself through another grinder.

metadata.title: Why the 34th St scope has to stay tight

artifacts.content:
Hi Ana,

Catching up between meetings and thought I’d actually answer your “how are you really?” text.

Work-wise, I’m neck-deep in this Oak Park Small Business Façade Improvement Project along the 34th Street corridor (3401–3459 34th, between 5th and 8th). It’s all the stuff you and I used to sketch in studio: repainting tired façades, swapping in energy-efficient windows and doors in existing openings, new signage and awnings that actually comply with the City’s sign ordinance, repairing sidewalks and accessible routes in the existing right-of-way, better wall-mounted and under-awning lighting, planter boxes and low-water shrubs in already disturbed spots. No new footprints, no additions, no change in use—just trying to make an old corridor feel cared for.

The equity piece is what’s keeping me going. Several of the participating businesses are minority- and women-owned, and I keep seeing my mom in them. You remember Rosa’s alterations shop on Stockton Boulevard? She had to close in June 2016 when she couldn’t get that $15,000 line of credit because of the medical debt from her 2012 knee replacement at UC Davis Medical Center. Every time I walk past the women-owned salon on 34th with its broken sign, I think, “Not again.”

On the home front, Elena is deep into her social justice phase—arguing about housing policy at dinner—and Mateo just wants more time at the Land Park soccer fields. Mamá’s arthritis has turned into a bigger thing since her small stroke last April; I’m at her Franklin Blvd apartment two evenings a week now. Some nights I feel like I’m held together with coffee and sticky notes.

We should schedule a call soon so I can hear about your latest project and complain properly about CEQA.

Abrazos,
Maria

Marisol: Can you talk?

Maria: Give me 5, just got out of a corridor meeting.

Marisol: Ok. Just worried about Mamá.

Maria: Same. Quick version: I’ve kept this 34th St Oak Park project super narrow on purpose. After that 2/7/22 settlement conference at the Schaber courthouse where the City Attorney walked us through how Mark got pulled into that old CEQA suit, I swore I’d stay in the categorical exemption lane—Class 1, 2, 3 only. So it’s just repainting façades, replacing doors/windows in existing openings, fixing sidewalks, adding lighting and planters. No digging up new ground, no intensifying uses.

Marisol: Is that why you said no to that guy wanting more repair bays?

Maria: Yep. Carlos called me on 3/12/23 about his friend at Oak Park Auto & Tire on 34th hoping we’d relax parking so he could expand. I told him I can’t tweak a City project to benefit one shop we know, especially as Senior Planner. Conflict-of-interest nightmare.

Marisol: Makes sense.

Maria: Plus, with Mamá’s stroke at Kaiser South Sac on 4/29/24 and me doing evenings at her Franklin Blvd apartment twice a week, I cannot take on an EIR-level monster. I’m barely keeping up with a 40–45 hr week as it is.

Marisol: I hate that she’s alone there.

Maria: Me too. Let’s talk this weekend about options—maybe rotating overnights or getting someone to come in a few mornings.

Marisol: Ok. Call me after the kids’ bedtime?

Maria: Around 9 works. Love you.

Marisol: Love you too.

Dear Ms. Patel,

Thank you again for meeting with me about Elena last week. I wanted to follow up with a bit more context that might be helpful as you check in with her this semester.

Elena has always been a sensitive kid, but her anxiety really spiked over the last couple of years. In October 2023, her therapist, Dr. Patricia Gomez at Kaiser South Sacramento, suggested we start doing short evening walks in safe, well-lit areas near home to help her manage things. Not long before that, she’d had a panic episode walking past the dim storefronts along the 34th Street corridor on our way to the Oak Park library. She still tenses up if we park too close to that stretch.

We also had a scare on March 18, 2023, when she had an asthma attack while walking home along the same corridor, near the McClatchy High area. She recovered, but it left all of us more cautious about air quality and long walks near busy streets.

Those experiences are part of why I’m so focused on improving lighting and sidewalks in Oak Park in my work with the City’s Community Development Department. I want the places she passes every day to feel safer and more predictable, not like a gauntlet.

At home, we’re trying to follow Dr. Gomez’s advice—keeping routines predictable, limiting late-night news, and using those breathing exercises Elena practiced in session. She may mention her abuela Rosa’s health too; my mom had a minor stroke last April and Elena worries about her a lot.

Please let me know if there are strategies you recommend we reinforce at home or if you notice particular triggers at school.

Best,
Maria Hernandez
(mom to Elena, 8th grade)

metadata.subject: Follow-up on Elena’s anxiety and health history

artifacts.content:
Event: Oak Park 34th St Corridor – Evening Walk & Debrief

Date: 2025-11-12
Time: 5:30–7:00 p.m.
Location: Meet at Oak Park Community Center, loop along 34th St between 5th–8th Ave, end near Broadway.
Attendees: Maria; Elena (if homework allows); possibly Javi.

Notes:
- Try to frame this as one of the short evening walks Dr. Patricia Gomez at Kaiser South Sacramento recommended back in Oct 2023—“safe, well-lit places in our own neighborhood”—to help with Elena’s anxiety.
- Be mindful that she had that panic episode walking past the dim 34th Street storefronts on the way to the Oak Park library; don’t push her to go farther than she wants.
- Also remember her asthma attack from 3/18/23 while walking home along this corridor near McClatchy High. Bring inhaler in my bag.
- Personally, this is a chance to reclaim that stretch after my own March 2022 experience being followed near 34th and Broadway and ending up at the 35th Street police substation to give a statement. Notice how the upgraded wall-mounted and under-awning lighting we’re proposing would change how it feels.
- Pay attention to storefronts like Luis Ramirez’s panadería and the women-owned salon—think about how fresh paint, new signage, and planters could help them the way my mom’s Stockton Blvd shop never got helped before it closed in June 2016.
- After walk: hot chocolate at home, quick debrief with Elena about what felt safe/unsafe, no heavy policy talk.
